EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe to extreme drought conditions at the end of April 2018 continued through May 2018. Severe drought expanded to nearly 90 percent of the state, of which 60 percent was deemed extreme drought or worse. Exceptional drought expanded to 18 percent of New Mexico. Spring runoff was nearly nonexistent at most river gages after record low snowpack at several key SNOTEL locations. Precipitation across central and western New Mexico was well below normal and temperatures were above normal. Spotty showers and thunderstorms impacted central and western New Mexico on a couple days however precipitation amounts were just a drop in the bucket compared to long-term deficits. Several back door frontal boundaries brought much needed showers and thunderstorms to parts of eastern New Mexico. Some areas of eastern New Mexico picked up between one and four inches of rainfall in May, along with a few bouts of severe hail and high winds. Some minor improvements were made to drought conditions along the Texas border. Periodic red flag conditions impacted nearly all of New Mexico during May 2018 which aided several new fire starts. The Buzzard Fire in western Catron County grew to over 30,000 acres after a hunter left a campfire abandoned. Nearly the entire state was in stage two fire restrictions and the entire Santa Fe National Forest was closed at the end of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from April deteriorated to extreme drought on May 8, 2018.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An exceptionally long stretch of dry weather over the southwest United States that began in early October allowed drought conditions to return to New Mexico by early January. Much of central and eastern New Mexico experienced wet conditions through the monsoon season into late September so drought was holding in the abnormally dry to moderate categories. Far western New Mexico did not experience as much precipitation through the same period so severe drought conditions returned to the area from Gallup southward toward the Gila Wilderness. A storm system on January 10, 2018 and another on January 20, 2018 brought some minor relief to the area but dry conditions returned quickly thereafter. These weak storm systems were no match for the extended period of well below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures for the state. Severe drought spread into over 60 percent of the state by the end of January 2018. Snowpack conditions were the worst ever recorded at several sites since the SNOTEL network was installed in the late 1970s and early 1980s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions were declared on January 23, 2018 for the Estancia Valley. Precipitation amounts were well below normal during the last three months of 2017 coupled with above normal temperatures. A couple quick-hitting, weak storm systems brought only light precipitation to the area in January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sufficient low level moisture and instability over eastern New Mexico on April 29th interacted with dry, southwest flow arriving from the west to produce the first significant dry line thunderstorms of the 2018 spring season. A cumulus field first developed along the area from near Ruidoso north to Santa Rosa and Harding County. Showers and thunderstorms began developing as the dry line moved eastward toward Roswell, Fort Sumner, and Tucumcari. A secondary area of showers and thunderstorms develop ahead of this line from Roosevelt County northward into Curry County. The most severe storms slammed the Clovis area with one to two inch hail, torrential rainfall, and gusty winds. Tucumcari also saw outflow wind gusts reach over 60 mph with significant blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tucumcari airport reported a peak thunderstorm wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shallow surge of low level moisture pushed west through the Albuquerque Metro Area and interacted with meager mid and upper level moisture that moved north from Mexico on April 28th. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed over the western high terrain of New Mexico that drifted east toward the late afternoon hours. A thunderstorm that dissipated several miles west of the Albuquerque metro forced an intense outflow boundary eastward into the the city. A sudden burst of winds near 60 mph on the west side of Albuquerque partially tore the roof off of a motel and snapped tree limbs. Blowing dust accompanied the outflow boundary across the metro area. Another dry thunderstorm near Las Vegas resulted in a peak wind gust up to 58 mph at the airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Las Vegas reported a peak wind gust up to 58 mph from a dry thunderstorm outflow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moving into western New Mexico combined with deep surface low pressure over eastern New Mexico to produce widespread, strong south to southwest winds over the state on May 2nd. Enough moisture and instability was present beneath the upper level low to generate scattered showers and thunderstorms. This activity brought the first measurable precipitation to several areas of central and western New Mexico in around five weeks. Showers and storms also enhanced strong winds across the area with many locations reporting peak wind gusts in the 45 to 55 mph range along with blowing dust. The strongest winds impacted the area from White Sands Missile Range northward to Clines Corners where peak gusts near 60 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clines Corners reported a peak wind gust up to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent back door cold front surged south and west through eastern New Mexico on May 19th and introduced much higher moisture across the eastern plains. Meanwhile, an upper level low pressure system strengthening over southern California spread very dry southwest flow over New Mexico. Sufficient moisture and instability were present over the plains by the late afternoon hours to produce a few severe thunderstorms with hail, high winds, heavy rainfall, and frequent lightning. The strongest storms impacted the area from Union County southward into eastern Guadalupe County. A storm northeast of Grenville produced severe wind gusts and up to five inches of hail. Another storm that passed north of Clayton produced severe wind gusts as it approached the Texas state line. Nickel size hail was reported along Interstate 40 at Newkirk. Several strong storms farther to the south between Tucumcari and Portales produced pea size hail, heavy rain, and strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size at the Newkirk service station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary sagged into far northeastern New Mexico on May 30th while very dry southwest flow aloft spread into the desert southwest ahead of an approaching upper level trough. Sufficient low level moisture and instability along the frontal boundary provided the focus for a few showers and thunderstorms over far northeastern New Mexico. A couple of these storms over Union County became strong to severe. The strongest storm impacted the area northwest of Sedan where quarter size hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters was found approximately 15 minutes after a storm passed over NM-562.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system centered over Arizona on May 21st drifted eastward toward the Four Corners region on May 22, 2018. Very dry southwest flow within the base of the upper low forced deeper moisture farther east into central and eastern New Mexico on the 22nd. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the central mountain chain during the early afternoon then spread north-northeast while intensifying over the high plains of eastern New Mexico through the evening. A large motor vehicle crash occurred on Interstate 25 near Santa Fe where significant accumulations of hail fell on the highway. Twelve vehicles were damaged or destroyed in the accident. Fortunately, no fatalities occurred from this incident. Storms continued to produce hail and high winds over eastern New Mexico through the overnight. Several storms that fired up over far southeastern Lincoln County and far western Chaves County produced quarter to hen egg size hail and minor flooding. A powerful storm over Quay County just after midnight tossed a sheet metal barn 100 yards and destroyed large sections of fencing and a nearby tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of tennis balls along U.S. Highway 380 north of San Patricio.

High temperatures in the 80s and 90s combined with very low humidity and strong winds set the stage for wildfires across the region. A prescribed burn between Bluewater Lake and Grants quickly spread out of control on the 12th while another wildfire developed from an abandoned camp fire. A grass fire that developed over extreme northern Lea County crossed into southeastern Roosevelt County near Milnesand and rapidly consumed nearly 40,000 acres. The 246 Fire near Capitan consumed nearly 4,000 acres as well as two homes and four outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gallup Municipal Airport reported peak sustained winds between 40 mph and 47 mph periodically for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first day of a two-day strong wind event began on April 12th as an unseasonably deep upper level storm system moved slowly east through the southern Rockies. Widespread, strong southwest winds increased over the region during the early morning hours of the 12th then peaked during the late afternoon and early evening hours as a potent cold front surged southeastward across the state. Widespread blowing dust was reported ahead of the cold front over the Four Corners region where hazardous visibilities were reported. A semi trailer was blown over along U.S. Highway 491 near Shiprock from high winds with blowing dust. The strongest winds were reported over central and western New Mexico where gusts of 60 to 70 mph were common. High temperatures in the 80s and 90s combined with very low humidity and strong winds set the stage for wildfires across the region. A prescribed burn between Bluewater Lake and Grants quickly spread out of control on the 12th while another wildfire developed from an abandoned camp fire. A grass fire that developed over extreme northern Lea County crossed into southeastern Roosevelt County near Milnesand and rapidly consumed nearly 40,000 acres. The 246 Fire near Capitan consumed nearly 4,000 acres as well as two homes and four outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: El Malpais RAWS site reported a peak wind gust up to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of moisture surged north and west into New Mexico on May 21, 2018 ahead of an upper level low pressure system approaching from Arizona. The combination of strong afternoon heating and moist instability allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop along the Continental Divide during the mid to late morning hours of the 21st. Hail, high winds, and funnel clouds were reported along the Continental Divide before spreading northeastward into the Rio Grande Valley during the early afternoon. A cluster of thunderstorms that developed just southwest of the Albuquerque Metro Area around 3 pm dumped copious amounts of hail and torrential rainfall across the city. Some parts of Albuquerque reported up to one inch of rain in less than 20 minutes. One woman was killed and five others injured when they were swept away in the urban drainage system designed to carry flood waters. These storms shifted east into the high plains of eastern New Mexico through the late afternoon and evening hours with more widespread severe weather. The Raton area also saw copious amounts of hail and heavy rainfall. Lincoln County was particularly hard hit with large hail up to the size of golf balls. Hail accumulated to at least one foot east of Alto as several severe storms moved over the same area. A funnel cloud was also spotted to the northeast of Ruidoso Downs. Heavy rainfall and small hail spread eastward into the plains through the overnight hours where many parts of eastern New Mexico picked up between one and two inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail near Candelaria Road and Carlisle Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sufficient low level moisture and instability over eastern New Mexico on April 29th interacted with dry, southwest flow arriving from the west to produce the first significant dry line thunderstorms of the 2018 spring season. A cumulus field first developed along the area from near Ruidoso north to Santa Rosa and Harding County. Showers and thunderstorms began developing as the dry line moved eastward toward Roswell, Fort Sumner, and Tucumcari. A secondary area of showers and thunderstorms develop ahead of this line from Roosevelt County northward into Curry County. The most severe storms slammed the Clovis area with one to two inch hail, torrential rainfall, and gusty winds. Tucumcari also saw outflow wind gusts reach over 60 mph with significant blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported in Clovis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe drought conditions at the end of March 2018 continued to deteriorate and expand in coverage through April 2018. Severe drought held constant at nearly 80 percent of the state, of which 46 percent was deemed extreme drought or worse. Exceptional drought expanded to nine percent of New Mexico for the first time since July 2014. Precipitation across the state was well below normal and temperatures were above normal as only a couple weak storm systems moved through the southwest United States. Strong winds occurred on many days during April 2018 with critical fire weather conditions on several consecutive days over large portions of the state. Blowing dust was also observed with several of these strong wind events. A couple very high elevation snow events dusted the northern high terrain however snowpack was essentially gone by the end of the month. Very little hydrologic response had been noted at several key runoff points within the Rio Grande Basin. Burn bans continued to expand in coverage over several areas of the state and the occurrence of small wildfires increased throughout the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from March deteriorated to extreme drought on April 10, 2018.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe to extreme drought conditions at the end of April 2018 continued through May 2018. Severe drought expanded to nearly 90 percent of the state, of which 60 percent was deemed extreme drought or worse. Exceptional drought expanded to 18 percent of New Mexico. Spring runoff was nearly nonexistent at most river gages after record low snowpack at several key SNOTEL locations. Precipitation across central and western New Mexico was well below normal and temperatures were above normal. Spotty showers and thunderstorms impacted central and western New Mexico on a couple days however precipitation amounts were just a drop in the bucket compared to long-term deficits. Several back door frontal boundaries brought much needed showers and thunderstorms to parts of eastern New Mexico. Some areas of eastern New Mexico picked up between one and four inches of rainfall in May, along with a few bouts of severe hail and high winds. Some minor improvements were made to drought conditions along the Texas border. Periodic red flag conditions impacted nearly all of New Mexico during May 2018 which aided several new fire starts. The Buzzard Fire in western Catron County grew to over 30,000 acres after a hunter left a campfire abandoned. Nearly the entire state was in stage two fire restrictions and the entire Santa Fe National Forest was closed at the end of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional drought conditions from April over parts of the county improved to extreme drought on May 29, 2018.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An exceptionally long stretch of dry weather over the southwest United States that began in early October allowed drought conditions to return to New Mexico by early January. Much of central and eastern New Mexico experienced wet conditions through the monsoon season into late September so drought was holding in the abnormally dry to moderate categories. Far western New Mexico did not experience as much precipitation through the same period so severe drought conditions returned to the area from Gallup southward toward the Gila Wilderness. A storm system on January 10, 2018 and another on January 20, 2018 brought some minor relief to the area but dry conditions returned quickly thereafter. These weak storm systems were no match for the extended period of well below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures for the state. Severe drought spread into over 60 percent of the state by the end of January 2018. Snowpack conditions were the worst ever recorded at several sites since the SNOTEL network was installed in the late 1970s and early 1980s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions were declared on January 23, 2018 for the Central Highlands. Precipitation amounts were well below normal during the last three months of 2017 coupled with above normal temperatures. A couple quick-hitting, weak storm systems brought only light precipitation to the area in January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist, back door frontal boundary shifted southwest into northeastern New Mexico during the early morning of May 15th. Meanwhile, a broad area of upper level low pressure over the Great Basin was spreading very dry, southwest flow over the remainder of New Mexico. Sufficient moisture and instability in this pattern allowed showers and thunderstorms to form along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains during the early afternoon hours. A few of these storms became strong to locally severe as they moved eastward across the eastern plains of New Mexico. The strongest cells moved through the area from eastern San Miguel County into Quay County where penny size hail and heavy rainfall was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail at Giddy Up Ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system moving through Colorado allowed scattered showers to move across far northern New Mexico during the late evening and overnight hours from April 7th into the 8th. A few of these showers raced eastward across San Juan County and allowed stronger winds aloft to mix to the surface. A peak wind gust up to 60 mph impacted the Farmington Regional Airport shortly before 11 pm on the 7th. These showers moved into the Sangre de Cristo Mountains during the overnight hours and produced an inch or two of snow at a couple SNOTELs. Kachina Peak at Taos Ski Valley reported a peak wind gust to 63 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain showers mixed stronger winds aloft to the surface resulting in a peak wind gust up to 60 mph at the Farmington Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system centered over Arizona on May 21st drifted eastward toward the Four Corners region on May 22, 2018. Very dry southwest flow within the base of the upper low forced deeper moisture farther east into central and eastern New Mexico on the 22nd. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the central mountain chain during the early afternoon then spread north-northeast while intensifying over the high plains of eastern New Mexico through the evening. A large motor vehicle crash occurred on Interstate 25 near Santa Fe where significant accumulations of hail fell on the highway. Twelve vehicles were damaged or destroyed in the accident. Fortunately, no fatalities occurred from this incident. Storms continued to produce hail and high winds over eastern New Mexico through the overnight. Several storms that fired up over far southeastern Lincoln County and far western Chaves County produced quarter to hen egg size hail and minor flooding. A powerful storm over Quay County just after midnight tossed a sheet metal barn 100 yards and destroyed large sections of fencing and a nearby tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail along NM-368 six miles south-southwest of Arabela.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably deep upper level storm system responsible for the widespread, strong southwest winds on April 12th exited slowly into the Great Plains through April 13th. Day two of the multi-day wind event cranked up early on the 13th as winds turned out of the northwest behind the exiting storm system. Blowing dust was confined to the far southeast plains and much of southern New Mexico. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common over nearly the entire area. High temperatures in the 80s and 90s from the 12th fell into the 40s and 50s on the 13th. These colder temperatures combined with strong winds made for a very chilly day. The wildfires that began on the 12th continued to burn through the 13th despite cooler temperatures and slightly higher humidity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dora mesonet reported sustained wind speeds between 40 mph and 43 mph periodically for a couple hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level storm system sliding east through the southern Rockies spread widespread strong to damaging winds across New Mexico on April 17th. Southwest winds increased quickly during the mid morning hours over western New Mexico before spreading eastward into the Rio Grande Valley and eastern plains. Peak wind gusts averaged 60 to 70 mph across the area. A large tree was blown over at an apartment complex near the Cottonwood Mall in Albuquerque. High winds blew down a power line near the Gallup airport. Wind gusts near 80 mph around Las Vegas caused part of the roof of a nearby church to blow off. Widespread blowing dust was also reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clayton reported peak wind gusts up to 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level pressure system approaching from the west combined with deepening surface low pressure over eastern New Mexico to produce widespread, strong southwest winds over the state on May 1st. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Smokey Bear RAWS reported a peak wind gust up to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For a third day in a row, and potentially the most widespread, severe weather impacted eastern New Mexico on May 23, 2018 as a strong dry line shifted east across the high plains. Showers and thunderstorms fired up just east of the central mountain chain by the mid to late afternoon hours then moved eastward on to the high plains through the early evening. A very impressive supercell storm over far southwestern Chaves County produced two tornadoes and large hail. Other storms in the area also produced severe hail and funnel clouds before merging into a large cluster of strong storms near Roswell. Another very impressive, long duration supercell storm over western San Miguel County produced torrential rainfall, flooding, high winds, and very large hail near Tecolotito and Anton Chico. Several other storms across the eastern plains were strong to severe with heavy rain, hail, and high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of golf balls 10 miles west of Roswell.

Another very impressive, long duration supercell storm over western San Miguel County produced torrential rainfall, flooding, high winds, and very large hail near Tecolotito and Anton Chico. Several other storms across the eastern plains were strong to severe with heavy rain, hail, and high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of golf balls two miles east of Mora.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moving south from Colorado intensified quickly over New Mexico on the evening of December 12th. A potent cold front in association with the upper level storm system moved rapidly southeast through central and western New Mexico shortly after sunset. Rain and snow showers along the cold front enhanced stronger wind gusts and many locations reported peak gusts between 45 and 55 mph. The back door portion of the cold front then surged south through eastern New Mexico during the early morning hours of the 13th and produced widespread wind gusts between 60 and 70 mph. Damage was reported in a couple areas of Quay and Curry counties. Light snowfall also occurred with this system as it intensified along the east slopes of the central mountain chain. Snowfall amounts ranged from two to six inches along the central high terrain and high plains of eastern New Mexico. Snow, blowing snow, reduced visibility, and icy travel conditions were reported during the morning commute. Interstate 25 at Raton Pass and U.S. 64 between Raton and Clayton were closed due to extremely icy travel with strong winds. Interstate 40 was also closed at Sedillo Hill for a brief period early in the morning due to a jack-knifed trailer and very slick travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Double Eagle Airport reported a peak wind gust to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shallow surge of low level moisture pushed west through the Albuquerque Metro Area and interacted with meager mid and upper level moisture that moved north from Mexico on April 28th. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed over the western high terrain of New Mexico that drifted east toward the late afternoon hours. A thunderstorm that dissipated several miles west of the Albuquerque metro forced an intense outflow boundary eastward into the the city. A sudden burst of winds near 60 mph on the west side of Albuquerque partially tore the roof off of a motel and snapped tree limbs. Blowing dust accompanied the outflow boundary across the metro area. Another dry thunderstorm near Las Vegas resulted in a peak wind gust up to 58 mph at the airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A five inch tree limb was snapped from a Cottonwood tree at an apartment complex near Alameda and Ellison. A large yucca cactus was also toppled on the west side of Albuquerque.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sufficient low level moisture and instability over eastern New Mexico on April 29th interacted with dry, southwest flow arriving from the west to produce the first significant dry line thunderstorms of the 2018 spring season. A cumulus field first developed along the area from near Ruidoso north to Santa Rosa and Harding County. Showers and thunderstorms began developing as the dry line moved eastward toward Roswell, Fort Sumner, and Tucumcari. A secondary area of showers and thunderstorms develop ahead of this line from Roosevelt County northward into Curry County. The most severe storms slammed the Clovis area with one to two inch hail, torrential rainfall, and gusty winds. Tucumcari also saw outflow wind gusts reach over 60 mph with significant blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of hail ranging from penny to ping pong ball size impacted the northeast side of Clovis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably deep upper level storm system responsible for the widespread, strong southwest winds on April 12th exited slowly into the Great Plains through April 13th. Day two of the multi-day wind event cranked up early on the 13th as winds turned out of the northwest behind the exiting storm system. Blowing dust was confined to the far southeast plains and much of southern New Mexico. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common over nearly the entire area. High temperatures in the 80s and 90s from the 12th fell into the 40s and 50s on the 13th. These colder temperatures combined with strong winds made for a very chilly day. The wildfires that began on the 12th continued to burn through the 13th despite cooler temperatures and slightly higher humidity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Columbia Scientific Balloon Facility reported sustained winds at Fort Sumner between 40 mph and 48 mph periodically for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first day of a two-day strong wind event began on April 12th as an unseasonably deep upper level storm system moved slowly east through the southern Rockies. Widespread, strong southwest winds increased over the region during the early morning hours of the 12th then peaked during the late afternoon and early evening hours as a potent cold front surged southeastward across the state. Widespread blowing dust was reported ahead of the cold front over the Four Corners region where hazardous visibilities were reported. A semi trailer was blown over along U.S. Highway 491 near Shiprock from high winds with blowing dust. The strongest winds were reported over central and western New Mexico where gusts of 60 to 70 mph were common. High temperatures in the 80s and 90s combined with very low humidity and strong winds set the stage for wildfires across the region. A prescribed burn between Bluewater Lake and Grants quickly spread out of control on the 12th while another wildfire developed from an abandoned camp fire. A grass fire that developed over extreme northern Lea County crossed into southeastern Roosevelt County near Milnesand and rapidly consumed nearly 40,000 acres. The 246 Fire near Capitan consumed nearly 4,000 acres as well as two homes and four outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sierra Blanca Regional Airport reported peak wind gusts between 58 mph and 66 mph periodically for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of moisture surged north and west into New Mexico on May 21, 2018 ahead of an upper level low pressure system approaching from Arizona. The combination of strong afternoon heating and moist instability allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop along the Continental Divide during the mid to late morning hours of the 21st. Hail, high winds, and funnel clouds were reported along the Continental Divide before spreading northeastward into the Rio Grande Valley during the early afternoon. A cluster of thunderstorms that developed just southwest of the Albuquerque Metro Area around 3 pm dumped copious amounts of hail and torrential rainfall across the city. Some parts of Albuquerque reported up to one inch of rain in less than 20 minutes. One woman was killed and five others injured when they were swept away in the urban drainage system designed to carry flood waters. These storms shifted east into the high plains of eastern New Mexico through the late afternoon and evening hours with more widespread severe weather. The Raton area also saw copious amounts of hail and heavy rainfall. Lincoln County was particularly hard hit with large hail up to the size of golf balls. Hail accumulated to at least one foot east of Alto as several severe storms moved over the same area. A funnel cloud was also spotted to the northeast of Ruidoso Downs. Heavy rainfall and small hail spread eastward into the plains through the overnight hours where many parts of eastern New Mexico picked up between one and two inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported near Menaul Boulevard and Juan Tabo Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level storm system sliding east through the southern Rockies spread widespread strong to damaging winds across New Mexico on April 17th. Southwest winds increased quickly during the mid morning hours over western New Mexico before spreading eastward into the Rio Grande Valley and eastern plains. Peak wind gusts averaged 60 to 70 mph across the area. A large tree was blown over at an apartment complex near the Cottonwood Mall in Albuquerque. High winds blew down a power line near the Gallup airport. Wind gusts near 80 mph around Las Vegas caused part of the roof of a nearby church to blow off. Widespread blowing dust was also reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mills Canyon RAWS reported a peak wind gust up to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe to extreme drought conditions at the end of April 2018 continued through May 2018. Severe drought expanded to nearly 90 percent of the state, of which 60 percent was deemed extreme drought or worse. Exceptional drought expanded to 18 percent of New Mexico. Spring runoff was nearly nonexistent at most river gages after record low snowpack at several key SNOTEL locations. Precipitation across central and western New Mexico was well below normal and temperatures were above normal. Spotty showers and thunderstorms impacted central and western New Mexico on a couple days however precipitation amounts were just a drop in the bucket compared to long-term deficits. Several back door frontal boundaries brought much needed showers and thunderstorms to parts of eastern New Mexico. Some areas of eastern New Mexico picked up between one and four inches of rainfall in May, along with a few bouts of severe hail and high winds. Some minor improvements were made to drought conditions along the Texas border. Periodic red flag conditions impacted nearly all of New Mexico during May 2018 which aided several new fire starts. The Buzzard Fire in western Catron County grew to over 30,000 acres after a hunter left a campfire abandoned. Nearly the entire state was in stage two fire restrictions and the entire Santa Fe National Forest was closed at the end of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions from April continued through May 2018.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe drought conditions at the end of March 2018 continued to deteriorate and expand in coverage through April 2018. Severe drought held constant at nearly 80 percent of the state, of which 46 percent was deemed extreme drought or worse. Exceptional drought expanded to nine percent of New Mexico for the first time since July 2014. Precipitation across the state was well below normal and temperatures were above normal as only a couple weak storm systems moved through the southwest United States. Strong winds occurred on many days during April 2018 with critical fire weather conditions on several consecutive days over large portions of the state. Blowing dust was also observed with several of these strong wind events. A couple very high elevation snow events dusted the northern high terrain however snowpack was essentially gone by the end of the month. Very little hydrologic response had been noted at several key runoff points within the Rio Grande Basin. Burn bans continued to expand in coverage over several areas of the state and the occurrence of small wildfires increased throughout the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from March continued through April 2018.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For a third day in a row, and potentially the most widespread, severe weather impacted eastern New Mexico on May 23, 2018 as a strong dry line shifted east across the high plains. Showers and thunderstorms fired up just east of the central mountain chain by the mid to late afternoon hours then moved eastward on to the high plains through the early evening. A very impressive supercell storm over far southwestern Chaves County produced two tornadoes and large hail. Other storms in the area also produced severe hail and funnel clouds before merging into a large cluster of strong storms near Roswell. Another very impressive, long duration supercell storm over western San Miguel County produced torrential rainfall, flooding, high winds, and very large hail near Tecolotito and Anton Chico. Several other storms across the eastern plains were strong to severe with heavy rain, hail, and high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters in Nara Visa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm moved over New Mexico causing strong westerly winds in the |Guadalupe Mountains, higher elevations of the Davis Mountains, and over Eddy County in |southeast New Mexico.

With cold air already in place from previous storm, a second deep upper low moved through the Four Corners region and south along the New Mexico/Arizona border region.  This system had a strong 120 knot jet associated with it and strong southwest upslope flow to bring over 2 feet of snow to parts of the Sacramento Mountains and 6 to 9 inches of snow to the Gila region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 6 inches of snow fell above 6000 feet in the Gila region during the event.  Snowfall reports ranged from about 6 inches 3 miles southeast of Silver City to 7.8 inches 4 miles northwest of Silver City with a maximum of 9.3 inches 5 miles west of Silver City.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES SACRAMENTO MOUNTAINS BELOW 7500 FEET

This system had a strong 120 knot jet associated with it and strong southwest upslope flow to bring over 2 feet of snow to parts of the Sacramento Mountains and 6 to 9 inches of snow to the Gila region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall of 16.0 inches was reported 6 miles east-northeast of La Luz at an elevation of 6100 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level low pressure system west of New Mexico spread strong south to southeast flow across the state on April 19th and 20th. Widespread southerly winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to 50 mph were reported across central and western New Mexico on the 19th. The strongest winds developed around the Santa Fe and Albuquerque metro areas after sunset when southeasterly winds increased to between 35 and 45 mph with gusts between 55 and 65 mph. Power lines were blown down in the Santa Fe resulting in numerous power outages. The east side of the Albuquerque area also saw power outages and traffic lights out through the night of the 19th. The storm system shifted east through the 20th and spread the stronger winds into eastern New Mexico where many areas saw southwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts near 55 mph. The first significant line of showers and thunderstorms of the spring season developed over the far eastern plains and spread heavy rainfall, small hail, and gusty winds over the area. Many folks in Union, Harding, Quay, Curry, and Roosevelt counties saw their heaviest rainfall in over six months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A ranch five miles southeast of Placitas reported peak wind gusts up to 67 mph.

A semi trailer was blown over along U.S. Highway 491 near Shiprock from high winds with blowing dust. The strongest winds were reported over central and western New Mexico where gusts of 60 to 70 mph were common. High temperatures in the 80s and 90s combined with very low humidity and strong winds set the stage for wildfires across the region. A prescribed burn between Bluewater Lake and Grants quickly spread out of control on the 12th while another wildfire developed from an abandoned camp fire. A grass fire that developed over extreme northern Lea County crossed into southeastern Roosevelt County near Milnesand and rapidly consumed nearly 40,000 acres. The 246 Fire near Capitan consumed nearly 4,000 acres as well as two homes and four outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station near Madrid reported a peak wind gust up to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of moisture surged north and west into New Mexico on May 21, 2018 ahead of an upper level low pressure system approaching from Arizona. The combination of strong afternoon heating and moist instability allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop along the Continental Divide during the mid to late morning hours of the 21st. Hail, high winds, and funnel clouds were reported along the Continental Divide before spreading northeastward into the Rio Grande Valley during the early afternoon. A cluster of thunderstorms that developed just southwest of the Albuquerque Metro Area around 3 pm dumped copious amounts of hail and torrential rainfall across the city. Some parts of Albuquerque reported up to one inch of rain in less than 20 minutes. One woman was killed and five others injured when they were swept away in the urban drainage system designed to carry flood waters. These storms shifted east into the high plains of eastern New Mexico through the late afternoon and evening hours with more widespread severe weather. The Raton area also saw copious amounts of hail and heavy rainfall. Lincoln County was particularly hard hit with large hail up to the size of golf balls. Hail accumulated to at least one foot east of Alto as several severe storms moved over the same area. A funnel cloud was also spotted to the northeast of Ruidoso Downs. Heavy rainfall and small hail spread eastward into the plains through the overnight hours where many parts of eastern New Mexico picked up between one and two inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of nickels accumulated two inches deep along U.S. Highway 54 southwest of Corona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe drought conditions at the end of March 2018 continued to deteriorate and expand in coverage through April 2018. Severe drought held constant at nearly 80 percent of the state, of which 46 percent was deemed extreme drought or worse. Exceptional drought expanded to nine percent of New Mexico for the first time since July 2014. Precipitation across the state was well below normal and temperatures were above normal as only a couple weak storm systems moved through the southwest United States. Strong winds occurred on many days during April 2018 with critical fire weather conditions on several consecutive days over large portions of the state. Blowing dust was also observed with several of these strong wind events. A couple very high elevation snow events dusted the northern high terrain however snowpack was essentially gone by the end of the month. Very little hydrologic response had been noted at several key runoff points within the Rio Grande Basin. Burn bans continued to expand in coverage over several areas of the state and the occurrence of small wildfires increased throughout the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions from March deteriorated to exceptional drought on April 17, 2018.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe to extreme drought conditions at the end of April 2018 continued through May 2018. Severe drought expanded to nearly 90 percent of the state, of which 60 percent was deemed extreme drought or worse. Exceptional drought expanded to 18 percent of New Mexico. Spring runoff was nearly nonexistent at most river gages after record low snowpack at several key SNOTEL locations. Precipitation across central and western New Mexico was well below normal and temperatures were above normal. Spotty showers and thunderstorms impacted central and western New Mexico on a couple days however precipitation amounts were just a drop in the bucket compared to long-term deficits. Several back door frontal boundaries brought much needed showers and thunderstorms to parts of eastern New Mexico. Some areas of eastern New Mexico picked up between one and four inches of rainfall in May, along with a few bouts of severe hail and high winds. Some minor improvements were made to drought conditions along the Texas border. Periodic red flag conditions impacted nearly all of New Mexico during May 2018 which aided several new fire starts. The Buzzard Fire in western Catron County grew to over 30,000 acres after a hunter left a campfire abandoned. Nearly the entire state was in stage two fire restrictions and the entire Santa Fe National Forest was closed at the end of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions from April deteriorated to exceptional drought on May 15, 2018. Several small wildfires started in the Jemez Mountains. The Santa Fe National Forest was closed at the end of May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For a third day in a row, and potentially the most widespread, severe weather impacted eastern New Mexico on May 23, 2018 as a strong dry line shifted east across the high plains. Showers and thunderstorms fired up just east of the central mountain chain by the mid to late afternoon hours then moved eastward on to the high plains through the early evening. A very impressive supercell storm over far southwestern Chaves County produced two tornadoes and large hail. Other storms in the area also produced severe hail and funnel clouds before merging into a large cluster of strong storms near Roswell. Another very impressive, long duration supercell storm over western San Miguel County produced torrential rainfall, flooding, high winds, and very large hail near Tecolotito and Anton Chico. Several other storms across the eastern plains were strong to severe with heavy rain, hail, and high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm chasers spotted a large wedge tornado on the ground for 15 to 20 minutes southwest of Picacho over rural southeastern Lincoln County. There is no access to this location and the strength of the tornado has been identified as unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An exceptionally long stretch of dry weather over the southwest United States that began in early October allowed drought conditions to return to New Mexico by early January. Much of central and eastern New Mexico experienced wet conditions through the monsoon season into late September so drought was holding in the abnormally dry to moderate categories. Far western New Mexico did not experience as much precipitation through the same period so severe drought conditions returned to the area from Gallup southward toward the Gila Wilderness. A storm system on January 10, 2018 and another on January 20, 2018 brought some minor relief to the area but dry conditions returned quickly thereafter. These weak storm systems were no match for the extended period of well below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures for the state. Severe drought spread into over 60 percent of the state by the end of January 2018. Snowpack conditions were the worst ever recorded at several sites since the SNOTEL network was installed in the late 1970s and early 1980s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions were declared for Roosevelt County on January 23, 2018. Most of this area had not received measurable precipitation in nearly four months. Patchy blowing dust had already been reported with a few of the stronger wind events by late January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A third winter storm in a series hammered much of New Mexico during the New Year's holiday with widespread snowfall, bitterly cold temperatures, and major travel impacts. Very cold air was already in place across the state behind the second storm that exited the area after Christmas. Another potent upper level gathered strength over the Four Corners region on New Year's Eve and moved slowly south and east into western New Mexico through New Year's Day. Widespread snowfall developed over northern and western New Mexico on New Year's Eve with significant travel impacts reported during evening holiday festivities. Gap winds on the east side of the Albuquerque metro area limited snowfall until winds subsided on New Years morning. The heaviest snow fell across the northern mountains where 12 to 18 inches was reported. The Jemez Mountains picked up 36 inches just west of Los Alamos. Bitterly cold air in place over the region with snow and blowing snow resulted in major travel problems with several road closures. Fatal accidents were reported on U.S. 550 and Interstate 40 in the Albuquerque area along with roughly 66 injury accidents in the city. Frigid temperatures resulted in damage to 40 schools in the Albuquerque area of which 25 experienced serious flood damage. Low temperatures on the day after New Years were the coldest since 2013 with many areas reporting readings below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts averaged around eight inches in the Farmington region. Severe travel conditions due to snow, low visibility, and frigid temperatures were reported across much of the area. At least three motor vehicle accidents were reported around Farmington but fortunately no injuries were reported. The city of Farmington extended shelter hours to assist those in need during the bitterly cold temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly winds mixed down in the Guadalupe Mountains, and portions of the southeast New Mexico plains, as the base of a potent upper trough swept over the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving trough of low pressure passing north of New Mexico on April 8th spread strong west to northwest winds across much of the region. Widespread wind gusts between 45 mph and 55 mph were reported over northern and central New Mexico. The strongest winds were reported along the east slopes of the central mountain chain where peak wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph occurred. Blowing dust was also reported with this event especially in dust prone locations, including near dry arroyo beds along Interstate 25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clines Corners reported peak sustained winds up to 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system centered over Arizona on May 21st drifted eastward toward the Four Corners region on May 22, 2018. Very dry southwest flow within the base of the upper low forced deeper moisture farther east into central and eastern New Mexico on the 22nd. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the central mountain chain during the early afternoon then spread north-northeast while intensifying over the high plains of eastern New Mexico through the evening. A large motor vehicle crash occurred on Interstate 25 near Santa Fe where significant accumulations of hail fell on the highway. Twelve vehicles were damaged or destroyed in the accident. Fortunately, no fatalities occurred from this incident. Storms continued to produce hail and high winds over eastern New Mexico through the overnight. Several storms that fired up over far southeastern Lincoln County and far western Chaves County produced quarter to hen egg size hail and minor flooding. A powerful storm over Quay County just after midnight tossed a sheet metal barn 100 yards and destroyed large sections of fencing and a nearby tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Interstate 25 southeast of Santa Fe and dumped up to four inches of hail. Slick travel conditions resulted in at least 12 motor vehicle crashes between mile markers 286 and 288. Photos from local media indicated several vehicles were totaled, however the extent of injuries is unknown. There were no known fatalities. Hail size and damage amounts have been estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level storm system sliding east through the southern Rockies spread widespread strong to damaging winds across New Mexico on April 17th. Southwest winds increased quickly during the mid morning hours over western New Mexico before spreading eastward into the Rio Grande Valley and eastern plains. Peak wind gusts averaged 60 to 70 mph across the area. A large tree was blown over at an apartment complex near the Cottonwood Mall in Albuquerque. High winds blew down a power line near the Gallup airport. Wind gusts near 80 mph around Las Vegas caused part of the roof of a nearby church to blow off. Widespread blowing dust was also reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Moriarty airport reported sustained winds between 40 mph and 45 mph periodically for a couple hours.

Precipitation amounts were near zero during the last three months of 2017 coupled with above normal temperatures. A couple quick-hitting, weak storm systems brought only very light precipitation to the area in January.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving upper level trough moving through the southern Rockies forced strong winds through the high terrain of central New Mexico and the adjacent eastern plains. West winds increased over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains during the early morning hours then peaked just after sunrise with gusts near 60 mph around Taos Ski Valley. Strong winds spread onto the east slopes and nearby high plains through the late morning hours before peaking during the early afternoon. Sustained winds of 35 to 45 mph with gusts between 50 and 60 mph were reported from near Angel Fire south to Las Vegas, Clines Corners, and the Sacramento Mountains. Very dry conditions with these winds and above normal temperatures generated critical fire weather over much of eastern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds between 40 and 44 mph were reported near Tierra Monte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving trough of low pressure passing north of New Mexico on April 8th spread strong west to northwest winds across much of the region. Widespread wind gusts between 45 mph and 55 mph were reported over northern and central New Mexico. The strongest winds were reported along the east slopes of the central mountain chain where peak wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph occurred. Blowing dust was also reported with this event especially in dust prone locations, including near dry arroyo beds along Interstate 25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Columbia Scientific Balloon Facility Transwestern Pump Station reported peak sustained winds between 40 mph and 51 mph for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sufficient low level moisture and instability over eastern New Mexico on April 29th interacted with dry, southwest flow arriving from the west to produce the first significant dry line thunderstorms of the 2018 spring season. A cumulus field first developed along the area from near Ruidoso north to Santa Rosa and Harding County. Showers and thunderstorms began developing as the dry line moved eastward toward Roswell, Fort Sumner, and Tucumcari. A secondary area of showers and thunderstorms develop ahead of this line from Roosevelt County northward into Curry County. The most severe storms slammed the Clovis area with one to two inch hail, torrential rainfall, and gusty winds. Tucumcari also saw outflow wind gusts reach over 60 mph with significant blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in Clovis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system centered over Arizona on May 21st drifted eastward toward the Four Corners region on May 22, 2018. Very dry southwest flow within the base of the upper low forced deeper moisture farther east into central and eastern New Mexico on the 22nd. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the central mountain chain during the early afternoon then spread north-northeast while intensifying over the high plains of eastern New Mexico through the evening. A large motor vehicle crash occurred on Interstate 25 near Santa Fe where significant accumulations of hail fell on the highway. Twelve vehicles were damaged or destroyed in the accident. Fortunately, no fatalities occurred from this incident. Storms continued to produce hail and high winds over eastern New Mexico through the overnight. Several storms that fired up over far southeastern Lincoln County and far western Chaves County produced quarter to hen egg size hail and minor flooding. A powerful storm over Quay County just after midnight tossed a sheet metal barn 100 yards and destroyed large sections of fencing and a nearby tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of ping pong balls 17 miles west of Roswell on U.S. Highway 70.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably deep upper level storm system responsible for the widespread, strong southwest winds on April 12th exited slowly into the Great Plains through April 13th. Day two of the multi-day wind event cranked up early on the 13th as winds turned out of the northwest behind the exiting storm system. Blowing dust was confined to the far southeast plains and much of southern New Mexico. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common over nearly the entire area. High temperatures in the 80s and 90s from the 12th fell into the 40s and 50s on the 13th. These colder temperatures combined with strong winds made for a very chilly day. The wildfires that began on the 12th continued to burn through the 13th despite cooler temperatures and slightly higher humidity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight Mile Draw RAWS reported peak wind gusts up to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first day of a two-day strong wind event began on April 12th as an unseasonably deep upper level storm system moved slowly east through the southern Rockies. Widespread, strong southwest winds increased over the region during the early morning hours of the 12th then peaked during the late afternoon and early evening hours as a potent cold front surged southeastward across the state. Widespread blowing dust was reported ahead of the cold front over the Four Corners region where hazardous visibilities were reported. A semi trailer was blown over along U.S. Highway 491 near Shiprock from high winds with blowing dust. The strongest winds were reported over central and western New Mexico where gusts of 60 to 70 mph were common. High temperatures in the 80s and 90s combined with very low humidity and strong winds set the stage for wildfires across the region. A prescribed burn between Bluewater Lake and Grants quickly spread out of control on the 12th while another wildfire developed from an abandoned camp fire. A grass fire that developed over extreme northern Lea County crossed into southeastern Roosevelt County near Milnesand and rapidly consumed nearly 40,000 acres. The 246 Fire near Capitan consumed nearly 4,000 acres as well as two homes and four outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station in Los Alamos reported a peak wind gust up to 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of moisture surged north and west into New Mexico on May 21, 2018 ahead of an upper level low pressure system approaching from Arizona. The combination of strong afternoon heating and moist instability allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop along the Continental Divide during the mid to late morning hours of the 21st. Hail, high winds, and funnel clouds were reported along the Continental Divide before spreading northeastward into the Rio Grande Valley during the early afternoon. A cluster of thunderstorms that developed just southwest of the Albuquerque Metro Area around 3 pm dumped copious amounts of hail and torrential rainfall across the city. Some parts of Albuquerque reported up to one inch of rain in less than 20 minutes. One woman was killed and five others injured when they were swept away in the urban drainage system designed to carry flood waters. These storms shifted east into the high plains of eastern New Mexico through the late afternoon and evening hours with more widespread severe weather. The Raton area also saw copious amounts of hail and heavy rainfall. Lincoln County was particularly hard hit with large hail up to the size of golf balls. Hail accumulated to at least one foot east of Alto as several severe storms moved over the same area. A funnel cloud was also spotted to the northeast of Ruidoso Downs. Heavy rainfall and small hail spread eastward into the plains through the overnight hours where many parts of eastern New Mexico picked up between one and two inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail near Southern Boulevard and Unser Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe to extreme drought conditions at the end of April 2018 continued through May 2018. Severe drought expanded to nearly 90 percent of the state, of which 60 percent was deemed extreme drought or worse. Exceptional drought expanded to 18 percent of New Mexico. Spring runoff was nearly nonexistent at most river gages after record low snowpack at several key SNOTEL locations. Precipitation across central and western New Mexico was well below normal and temperatures were above normal. Spotty showers and thunderstorms impacted central and western New Mexico on a couple days however precipitation amounts were just a drop in the bucket compared to long-term deficits. Several back door frontal boundaries brought much needed showers and thunderstorms to parts of eastern New Mexico. Some areas of eastern New Mexico picked up between one and four inches of rainfall in May, along with a few bouts of severe hail and high winds. Some minor improvements were made to drought conditions along the Texas border. Periodic red flag conditions impacted nearly all of New Mexico during May 2018 which aided several new fire starts. The Buzzard Fire in western Catron County grew to over 30,000 acres after a hunter left a campfire abandoned. Nearly the entire state was in stage two fire restrictions and the entire Santa Fe National Forest was closed at the end of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions from April continued through May 2018.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET/RED RIVER
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe drought conditions at the end of March 2018 continued to deteriorate and expand in coverage through April 2018. Severe drought held constant at nearly 80 percent of the state, of which 46 percent was deemed extreme drought or worse. Exceptional drought expanded to nine percent of New Mexico for the first time since July 2014. Precipitation across the state was well below normal and temperatures were above normal as only a couple weak storm systems moved through the southwest United States. Strong winds occurred on many days during April 2018 with critical fire weather conditions on several consecutive days over large portions of the state. Blowing dust was also observed with several of these strong wind events. A couple very high elevation snow events dusted the northern high terrain however snowpack was essentially gone by the end of the month. Very little hydrologic response had been noted at several key runoff points within the Rio Grande Basin. Burn bans continued to expand in coverage over several areas of the state and the occurrence of small wildfires increased throughout the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions from March continued through April 2018.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For a third day in a row, and potentially the most widespread, severe weather impacted eastern New Mexico on May 23, 2018 as a strong dry line shifted east across the high plains. Showers and thunderstorms fired up just east of the central mountain chain by the mid to late afternoon hours then moved eastward on to the high plains through the early evening. A very impressive supercell storm over far southwestern Chaves County produced two tornadoes and large hail. Other storms in the area also produced severe hail and funnel clouds before merging into a large cluster of strong storms near Roswell. Another very impressive, long duration supercell storm over western San Miguel County produced torrential rainfall, flooding, high winds, and very large hail near Tecolotito and Anton Chico. Several other storms across the eastern plains were strong to severe with heavy rain, hail, and high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very quick storm produced hail up to the size of nickels for less than one minute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level low pressure system west of New Mexico spread strong south to southeast flow across the state on April 19th and 20th. Widespread southerly winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to 50 mph were reported across central and western New Mexico on the 19th. The strongest winds developed around the Santa Fe and Albuquerque metro areas after sunset when southeasterly winds increased to between 35 and 45 mph with gusts between 55 and 65 mph. Power lines were blown down in the Santa Fe resulting in numerous power outages. The east side of the Albuquerque area also saw power outages and traffic lights out through the night of the 19th. The storm system shifted east through the 20th and spread the stronger winds into eastern New Mexico where many areas saw southwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts near 55 mph. The first significant line of showers and thunderstorms of the spring season developed over the far eastern plains and spread heavy rainfall, small hail, and gusty winds over the area. Many folks in Union, Harding, Quay, Curry, and Roosevelt counties saw their heaviest rainfall in over six months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station near Madrid reported peak wind gusts between 58 mph and 65 mph periodically for a couple hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moving south from Colorado intensified quickly over New Mexico on the evening of December 12th. A potent cold front in association with the upper level storm system moved rapidly southeast through central and western New Mexico shortly after sunset. Rain and snow showers along the cold front enhanced stronger wind gusts and many locations reported peak gusts between 45 and 55 mph. The back door portion of the cold front then surged south through eastern New Mexico during the early morning hours of the 13th and produced widespread wind gusts between 60 and 70 mph. Damage was reported in a couple areas of Quay and Curry counties. Light snowfall also occurred with this system as it intensified along the east slopes of the central mountain chain. Snowfall amounts ranged from two to six inches along the central high terrain and high plains of eastern New Mexico. Snow, blowing snow, reduced visibility, and icy travel conditions were reported during the morning commute. Interstate 25 at Raton Pass and U.S. 64 between Raton and Clayton were closed due to extremely icy travel with strong winds. Interstate 40 was also closed at Sedillo Hill for a brief period early in the morning due to a jack-knifed trailer and very slick travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clayton airport reported peak wind gusts between 58 mph and 63 mph for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level storm system sliding east through the southern Rockies spread widespread strong to damaging winds across New Mexico on April 17th. Southwest winds increased quickly during the mid morning hours over western New Mexico before spreading eastward into the Rio Grande Valley and eastern plains. Peak wind gusts averaged 60 to 70 mph across the area. A large tree was blown over at an apartment complex near the Cottonwood Mall in Albuquerque. High winds blew down a power line near the Gallup airport. Wind gusts near 80 mph around Las Vegas caused part of the roof of a nearby church to blow off. Widespread blowing dust was also reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Angel Fire airport reported a peak wind gust up to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level low pressure system nearly stationary over the Great Basin provided strong southwest flow over New Mexico on May 11th. Much of central and western New Mexico experienced peak wind gusts in the 40 to 50 mph range. The strongest winds occurred around Gallup where peak wind gusts topped out between 58 mph and 62 mph periodically through the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gallup reported a peak wind gust up to 59 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Columbia Scientific Balloon Facility Transwestern Pump station reported sustained wind speeds between 40 mph and 51 mph periodically for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe drought conditions at the end of March 2018 continued to deteriorate and expand in coverage through April 2018. Severe drought held constant at nearly 80 percent of the state, of which 46 percent was deemed extreme drought or worse. Exceptional drought expanded to nine percent of New Mexico for the first time since July 2014. Precipitation across the state was well below normal and temperatures were above normal as only a couple weak storm systems moved through the southwest United States. Strong winds occurred on many days during April 2018 with critical fire weather conditions on several consecutive days over large portions of the state. Blowing dust was also observed with several of these strong wind events. A couple very high elevation snow events dusted the northern high terrain however snowpack was essentially gone by the end of the month. Very little hydrologic response had been noted at several key runoff points within the Rio Grande Basin. Burn bans continued to expand in coverage over several areas of the state and the occurrence of small wildfires increased throughout the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions from March continued through April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe to extreme drought conditions at the end of April 2018 continued through May 2018. Severe drought expanded to nearly 90 percent of the state, of which 60 percent was deemed extreme drought or worse. Exceptional drought expanded to 18 percent of New Mexico. Spring runoff was nearly nonexistent at most river gages after record low snowpack at several key SNOTEL locations. Precipitation across central and western New Mexico was well below normal and temperatures were above normal. Spotty showers and thunderstorms impacted central and western New Mexico on a couple days however precipitation amounts were just a drop in the bucket compared to long-term deficits. Several back door frontal boundaries brought much needed showers and thunderstorms to parts of eastern New Mexico. Some areas of eastern New Mexico picked up between one and four inches of rainfall in May, along with a few bouts of severe hail and high winds. Some minor improvements were made to drought conditions along the Texas border. Periodic red flag conditions impacted nearly all of New Mexico during May 2018 which aided several new fire starts. The Buzzard Fire in western Catron County grew to over 30,000 acres after a hunter left a campfire abandoned. Nearly the entire state was in stage two fire restrictions and the entire Santa Fe National Forest was closed at the end of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional drought conditions from April continued through May 2018.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For a third day in a row, and potentially the most widespread, severe weather impacted eastern New Mexico on May 23, 2018 as a strong dry line shifted east across the high plains. Showers and thunderstorms fired up just east of the central mountain chain by the mid to late afternoon hours then moved eastward on to the high plains through the early evening. A very impressive supercell storm over far southwestern Chaves County produced two tornadoes and large hail. Other storms in the area also produced severe hail and funnel clouds before merging into a large cluster of strong storms near Roswell. Another very impressive, long duration supercell storm over western San Miguel County produced torrential rainfall, flooding, high winds, and very large hail near Tecolotito and Anton Chico. Several other storms across the eastern plains were strong to severe with heavy rain, hail, and high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second thunderstorm southwest of Picacho produced what another tornado within rural southeastern Lincoln County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second punch of winter weather struck New Mexico shortly after Christmas as a slow-moving storm system shifted southeast through the Four Corners region. A potent back door cold front over eastern New Mexico set the stage for easterly upslope flow along the central mountain chain and strong gap winds in the Rio Grande Valley. Snow developed over western New Mexico on the afternoon of the 27th as the storm system approached from the Four Corners. Snow spread into the Rio Grande Valley and central mountain chain by the evening hours. The most significant portion of the storm blasted the region on the 28th as the storm system moving in from the west interacted with the very cold and moist airmass in place over eastern New Mexico. Widespread heavy snowfall amounts of 12 to 18 were reported with upwards of 30 inches in the Ruidoso area. The Albuquerque metro area and the Sandia/Manzano mountains saw periods of blizzard conditions with high winds. Widespread difficult to severe travel occurred over the area with road closures and stranded motorists.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from three to six inches in the area from Gallup to Vanderwagon. Difficult travel conditions were reported by NMDOT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An exceptionally long stretch of dry weather over the southwest United States that began in early October allowed drought conditions to return to New Mexico by early January. Much of central and eastern New Mexico experienced wet conditions through the monsoon season into late September so drought was holding in the abnormally dry to moderate categories. Far western New Mexico did not experience as much precipitation through the same period so severe drought conditions returned to the area from Gallup southward toward the Gila Wilderness. A storm system on January 10, 2018 and another on January 20, 2018 brought some minor relief to the area but dry conditions returned quickly thereafter. These weak storm systems were no match for the extended period of well below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures for the state. Severe drought spread into over 60 percent of the state by the end of January 2018. Snowpack conditions were the worst ever recorded at several sites since the SNOTEL network was installed in the late 1970s and early 1980s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions were declared on January 23, 2018 for the West Central Mountains from near McGaffey to El Malpais and the Zuni Mountains. Precipitation amounts were near zero over the area during the last three months of 2017 with above normal temperatures. Snowpack in the high terrain was an abysmal 16 percent of normal by the end of January 2018. A couple quick-hitting, weak storm systems brought only very light precipitation to the area in early to mid January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level low pressure system west of New Mexico spread strong south to southeast flow across the state on April 19th and 20th. Widespread southerly winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to 50 mph were reported across central and western New Mexico on the 19th. The strongest winds developed around the Santa Fe and Albuquerque metro areas after sunset when southeasterly winds increased to between 35 and 45 mph with gusts between 55 and 65 mph. Power lines were blown down in the Santa Fe resulting in numerous power outages. The east side of the Albuquerque area also saw power outages and traffic lights out through the night of the 19th. The storm system shifted east through the 20th and spread the stronger winds into eastern New Mexico where many areas saw southwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts near 55 mph. The first significant line of showers and thunderstorms of the spring season developed over the far eastern plains and spread heavy rainfall, small hail, and gusty winds over the area. Many folks in Union, Harding, Quay, Curry, and Roosevelt counties saw their heaviest rainfall in over six months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two power poles were blown down on Interstate 25 near mile marker 245. Cost of the damage is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moving south from Colorado intensified quickly over New Mexico on the evening of December 12th. A potent cold front in association with the upper level storm system moved rapidly southeast through central and western New Mexico shortly after sunset. Rain and snow showers along the cold front enhanced stronger wind gusts and many locations reported peak gusts between 45 and 55 mph. The back door portion of the cold front then surged south through eastern New Mexico during the early morning hours of the 13th and produced widespread wind gusts between 60 and 70 mph. Damage was reported in a couple areas of Quay and Curry counties. Light snowfall also occurred with this system as it intensified along the east slopes of the central mountain chain. Snowfall amounts ranged from two to six inches along the central high terrain and high plains of eastern New Mexico. Snow, blowing snow, reduced visibility, and icy travel conditions were reported during the morning commute. Interstate 25 at Raton Pass and U.S. 64 between Raton and Clayton were closed due to extremely icy travel with strong winds. Interstate 40 was also closed at Sedillo Hill for a brief period early in the morning due to a jack-knifed trailer and very slick travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raton airport reported peak wind gusts between 58 mph and 69 mph for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first day of a two-day strong wind event began on April 12th as an unseasonably deep upper level storm system moved slowly east through the southern Rockies. Widespread, strong southwest winds increased over the region during the early morning hours of the 12th then peaked during the late afternoon and early evening hours as a potent cold front surged southeastward across the state. Widespread blowing dust was reported ahead of the cold front over the Four Corners region where hazardous visibilities were reported. A semi trailer was blown over along U.S. Highway 491 near Shiprock from high winds with blowing dust. The strongest winds were reported over central and western New Mexico where gusts of 60 to 70 mph were common. High temperatures in the 80s and 90s combined with very low humidity and strong winds set the stage for wildfires across the region. A prescribed burn between Bluewater Lake and Grants quickly spread out of control on the 12th while another wildfire developed from an abandoned camp fire. A grass fire that developed over extreme northern Lea County crossed into southeastern Roosevelt County near Milnesand and rapidly consumed nearly 40,000 acres. The 246 Fire near Capitan consumed nearly 4,000 acres as well as two homes and four outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Columbia Scientific Balloon Facility Transwestern Pump station reported sustained winds between 40 mph and 47 mph periodically for several hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Farmington Four Corners Regional Airport reported peak sustained winds between 40 mph and 47 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of moisture surged north and west into New Mexico on May 21, 2018 ahead of an upper level low pressure system approaching from Arizona. The combination of strong afternoon heating and moist instability allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop along the Continental Divide during the mid to late morning hours of the 21st. Hail, high winds, and funnel clouds were reported along the Continental Divide before spreading northeastward into the Rio Grande Valley during the early afternoon. A cluster of thunderstorms that developed just southwest of the Albuquerque Metro Area around 3 pm dumped copious amounts of hail and torrential rainfall across the city. Some parts of Albuquerque reported up to one inch of rain in less than 20 minutes. One woman was killed and five others injured when they were swept away in the urban drainage system designed to carry flood waters. These storms shifted east into the high plains of eastern New Mexico through the late afternoon and evening hours with more widespread severe weather. The Raton area also saw copious amounts of hail and heavy rainfall. Lincoln County was particularly hard hit with large hail up to the size of golf balls. Hail accumulated to at least one foot east of Alto as several severe storms moved over the same area. A funnel cloud was also spotted to the northeast of Ruidoso Downs. Heavy rainfall and small hail spread eastward into the plains through the overnight hours where many parts of eastern New Mexico picked up between one and two inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media shared imagery of a funnel cloud near Datil on Twitter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level storm system sliding east through the southern Rockies spread widespread strong to damaging winds across New Mexico on April 17th. Southwest winds increased quickly during the mid morning hours over western New Mexico before spreading eastward into the Rio Grande Valley and eastern plains. Peak wind gusts averaged 60 to 70 mph across the area. A large tree was blown over at an apartment complex near the Cottonwood Mall in Albuquerque. High winds blew down a power line near the Gallup airport. Wind gusts near 80 mph around Las Vegas caused part of the roof of a nearby church to blow off. Widespread blowing dust was also reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clines Corners reported peak wind gusts between 58 mph and 70 mph for several hours.
